stories.title: Amazon Q Developer agentic capabilities on SWE-Bench
stories.summary: Amazon Q Developer's agentic capabilities claimed top positions on both SWE-Bench and SWE-Bench Lite leaderboards, which evaluate AI systems on real GitHub issues requiring multi-file edits across open-source Python repositories. SWE-Bench measures an agent's ability to autonomously resolve issues — writing code, running tests, and iterating — without human guidance, making it a more meaningful proxy for real dev work than single-turn coding benchmarks. The specific resolve rates aren't disclosed in this announcement (the actual leaderboard numbers are the operative metric here, not Amazon's marketing copy), so engineers should check swebench.com directly for the verified scores. In practice, this means Q Developer's agent can now be benchmarked against Claude, GPT-4o, and open-source agents like SWE-agent on a common yardstick — giving teams a data point beyond vendor demos when evaluating AI coding assistants.
stories.considerations: On a concrete workflow: if you're using Q Developer's '/dev' agentic mode in VS Code or JetBrains to resolve a bug spanning 5–10 files in a mid-sized Java or Python service, SWE-Bench performance correlates with whether the agent correctly identifies root cause, edits the right files, and passes existing tests without manual steering — the exact failure mode that makes current AI agents frustrating in practice. The non-obvious implication: SWE-Bench is Python-heavy and skews toward well-tested OSS repos with clear issue descriptions — performance on your internal TypeScript monorepo with sparse test coverage and ambiguous tickets will likely diverge significantly from benchmark numbers. This announcement lands as the agentic coding space is getting crowded fast (Cursor, Copilot Workspace, Devin, SWE-agent), and AWS needs a credibility signal beyond 'it's integrated with your existing IAM' — a leaderboard top spot is a legitimate differentiator if the numbers hold up to scrutiny. The honest gotcha: Amazon hasn't published the specific resolve-rate percentages in this release, which is a red flag — top leaderboard entries typically lead with the number (e.g., '48.6% on SWE-Bench Verified'), and omitting it here suggests either the margin is thin or the submission category is narrower than the headline implies; verify the methodology on the leaderboard directly before citing this in any internal tooling decision.

stories.title: Composer 2.5 Model Release
stories.summary: Cursor released Composer 2.5, an updated agentic coding model replacing Composer 2. The model ships in two tiers: Standard at $0.50/M input and $2.50/M output tokens, and Fast at $3.00/M input and $15.00/M output tokens — a 6x price differential suggesting meaningfully different inference infrastructure or routing. The changelog claims improvements in sustained multi-step task execution and instruction adherence, though no specific benchmarks, context window sizes, or latency figures are disclosed. Developers can now run longer autonomous coding sessions with reportedly fewer instruction-drift failures compared to Composer 2.
stories.considerations: The most concrete scenario where this matters: multi-file refactors where Composer 2 would silently drop constraints mid-session — say, renaming a pattern across 50+ files while respecting a specific naming convention — are the stated target for the 'follows complex instructions more reliably' claim, though without published evals this is hard to verify independently. The 6x price gap between Standard and Fast tiers is the non-obvious detail: this implies Fast is likely routing to a larger or less-quantized model, and teams building cost-sensitive pipelines need to audit which tier their Cursor plan defaults to before assuming budget behavior. This release lands as agentic coding sessions are getting longer and more autonomous, directly addressing the well-documented failure mode where models lose constraint fidelity after many sequential tool calls. The honest gotcha: 'more pleasant to collaborate with' is doing a lot of work in the changelog with zero specifics — tone and verbosity changes in AI coding assistants can subtly alter diff quality and comment density in ways that require teams to re-calibrate code review expectations.

stories.title: Claude Opus 4.7 fast mode now available
stories.summary: Windsurf has added a 'fast mode' variant of Claude Opus 4.7 to its platform, delivering approximately 2.5x higher output token throughput compared to standard Opus 4.7. The speed increase likely comes from infrastructure-level optimizations — speculative decoding, batching strategies, or routing to higher-throughput hardware — rather than model quantization or capability reduction, since Windsurf explicitly claims full reasoning capabilities are preserved. No changes to context window size or model weights are indicated. Developers can now use Opus 4.7's full intelligence tier for latency-sensitive tasks like inline completions and rapid iteration loops that previously required dropping down to a faster but less capable model.
stories.considerations: In a tight edit-test-debug loop on a large TypeScript monorepo, the 2.5x throughput gain could meaningfully compress the wait time for multi-file refactors or long generated diffs — the kind of output where standard Opus 4.7 might take 15-20 seconds and fast mode could cut that under 8. The non-obvious implication: if fast mode is available at the same credit cost as standard mode, it will likely become the default choice for most users, which could shift Windsurf's infrastructure load significantly and potentially affect availability or rate limits under heavy demand. This addresses a real and persistent complaint — that frontier-tier models are too slow for interactive coding workflows, forcing developers to use weaker models just to maintain flow state. The honest gotcha: 'full reasoning capabilities preserved' is a marketing claim without a benchmark to back it up; speculative decoding and similar techniques can introduce subtle output distribution shifts, so teams doing precision-sensitive work like code generation with strict correctness requirements should validate outputs before fully trusting the equivalence claim.

stories.title: Filter session history by workspace directory
stories.summary: Continue.dev added workspace-directory filtering to session history, letting users scope the chat history list to sessions initiated from a specific folder. The filter works by associating each session record with the workspace root at creation time, then matching against that stored path when the filter is applied. No context-window or API changes are involved — this is purely a history-index UI feature. Developers who switch between multiple repos in the same Continue instance can now pull up only the sessions relevant to a given project without scrolling through unrelated conversations.
stories.considerations: Practically, if you maintain separate workspaces for, say, a monorepo backend and a React frontend, you can now open the backend folder and immediately see only the debugging sessions from that context — no more hunting through 50 mixed sessions to find the one where you traced that gRPC timeout. The non-obvious implication: session metadata is now path-coupled, so renaming or moving a workspace directory will likely orphan those sessions from the filter, leaving them only visible in the unfiltered global view. This lands at a moment when developers are running more parallel AI-assisted workstreams across projects, and global session history has become a real UX bottleneck as usage scales up. Worth noting: sessions created before this release won't have workspace metadata attached, so the filter will only be useful for new sessions going forward — existing history stays in the unfiltered pile.

stories.title: Claude Code Hooks: Synchronous Middleware for Every AI Tool Call
stories.summary: Claude Code Hooks adds synchronous middleware injection at 20+ named lifecycle points in the agentic loop — SessionStart, PreToolUse, PostToolBatch, Stop, and others — letting developers intercept every tool call before it touches the filesystem. Hooks are shell commands, HTTP endpoints, or LLM prompts that receive full JSON context on stdin and return structured decisions (block, approve, retry, mutate) via exit codes and JSON on stdout. The critical mechanism: a PreToolUse hook returning exit code 2 pipes its stderr directly into Claude's context as a model-visible error, meaning the model reads your policy violation message and self-corrects rather than hitting a silent hard wall. You can now enforce schema validation, secret scanning, cost controls, or custom approval gates that Claude actively reasons about and adapts to — not just guardrails that terminate execution.
stories.considerations: In a 300-file monorepo where Claude is doing autonomous refactoring, you can attach a PreToolUse hook that runs a fast AST linter on every proposed Write call — if the hook returns exit code 2 with 'imports org.deprecated.* detected', Claude sees that message, understands the constraint, and rewrites the file before it ever lands on disk, eliminating a whole class of post-hoc review cycles. The non-obvious second-order effect: because exit code 2 feeds stderr back as model context, you can write hooks that are themselves LLM prompts — effectively creating a meta-reasoning layer where one model audits another's tool calls in real time, which opens the door to cheap specialized policy models running inline. This lands at exactly the moment teams are moving from 'Claude as assistant' to 'Claude as autonomous agent' and discovering that silent sub-agents failing quietly is their biggest reliability problem — hooks give you observable, enforceable checkpoints without forking the core loop. The gotcha worth flagging: hooks run synchronously on every tool call, so a slow HTTP endpoint or a heavy LLM-as-hook will directly multiply your wall-clock latency — a 500ms policy check on a 200-tool session adds 100 seconds of dead time, so you need to be disciplined about keeping hot-path hooks sub-50ms or gating expensive checks behind tool-name filters.
